title: Decaying HyperLogLog: Continuous-Time Cardinality Estimation with Exponential Aging
description: We propose the Decaying HyperLogLog (D-HLL) algorithm, the first cardinality estimator that supports continuous exponential decay of element contributions over time while maintaining mergeability for distributed computation. Unlike sliding window approaches that abruptly discard old data, D-HLL allows the influence of each element to decay smoothly and exponentially with its age, enabling more natural modeling of information aging in real-time analytics, network monitoring, and fraud detection.
motivation: Current cardinality estimation approaches treat all historical data equally (landmark model) or abruptly cut off data older than a fixed window (sliding window model). Neither is suitable for applications where recent data should be weighted more heavily but old data should still contribute gradually decaying information. Exponential decay models are widely used in frequency estimation but have not been successfully integrated into cardinality estimation due to the non-linear nature of distinct counting. This work fills that gap.
proposed_approach: D-HLL extends HyperLogLog by storing decaying register values that decrease exponentially over time. Each register M[j] stores the maximum of e^(-λ(t - t_x)) · ρ(h(x)) for elements x mapping to bucket j, where λ is the decay rate and t_x is the arrival time. We use lazy decay (applying decay only on register access) for efficiency. The estimator uses the standard HLL formula with decayed register values. Mergeability is preserved by synchronizing timestamps and taking the maximum of decayed values from each sketch.
key_innovations: First cardinality estimator with continuous exponential decay
Lazy decay mechanism enabling O(1) amortized update time
hypothesis: The Decaying HyperLogLog algorithm provides an asymptotically unbiased estimate of exponentially decayed cardinality with relative standard error O(1/√m), matching standard HyperLogLog accuracy while supporting continuous-time decay semantics and maintaining mergeability.

confirmed: Relative bias < 5% across decay rates λ ∈ [0.001, 0.1]
Relative standard error scales as O(1/√m) and remains < 2% for m = 2048
Throughput > 10^6 elements/second on single CPU core
refuted: Exponential decay introduces unbounded bias in cardinality estimation
Space or time overhead exceeds 10x compared to standard HLL

related_work.paper: HyperLogLog: the analysis of a near-optimal cardinality estimation algorithm (Flajolet et al., 2007)
related_work.relationship: Foundation of our work. D-HLL extends HLL with decay semantics while preserving its core stochastic averaging principle.

related_work.paper: Sliding HyperLogLog: Estimating cardinality in a data stream over a sliding window (Chabchoub et al., 2010)
related_work.relationship: Closest existing work. Uses hard sliding window with LPFM data structure. D-HLL provides continuous decay with less space and smoother transitions.

related_work.paper: UltraLogLog: A practical and more space-efficient alternative to HyperLogLog (Ertl, 2024)
related_work.relationship: Recent space optimization for standard HLL. Our work is orthogonal—decay semantics could be applied to UltraLogLog as well.

related_work.paper: Sketch-based querying of distributed sliding-window data streams (Papapetrou et al., 2012)
related_work.relationship: ECM-Sketch for frequency estimation with sliding windows. D-HLL targets cardinality estimation with exponential (not sliding window) decay.

related_work.paper: Forward decay: A practical time decay model for streaming systems (Cormode et al., 2009)
related_work.relationship: Introduces forward decay for aggregates. We apply similar concepts specifically to cardinality estimation.

datasets: Synthetic uniform and Zipfian streams (10^7 - 10^9 elements)

feasibility.computational_requirements: CPU-only, 2 cores, 128GB RAM sufficient. No GPU needed.
feasibility.time_estimate: ~8 hours for implementation and all experiments
feasibility.why_feasible: Pure algorithmic work with analytical and systems experiments. No model training or GPU computation required. Implementation in C++/Python with moderate codebase (~1500 lines).

potential_venues: SIGMOD: ACM Conference on Management of Data
VLDB: International Conference on Very Large Data Bases
PODS: ACM Symposium on Principles of Database Systems
ICDE: IEEE International Conference on Data Engineering

risks_and_mitigations.mitigation: Fallback to bounded decay functions; theoretical analysis of bias bounds
Quantized timestamps and batch decay optimizations
Periodic background decay sweeps; adaptive decay scheduling
